content:
5 min read Just now -- Press enter or click to view image in full size Stop Writing REST APIs Just to Feed Your Frontend Table ComponentAt some point, the modern B2B frontend stack stopped making sense and nobody filed a bug report. Here’s what a typical data table in a React dashboard looks like today, traced from the moment a user clicks a filter to the moment pixels appear on screen: • User interaction triggers a state update. • React re-renders the component with a loading state. • A fetch call goes out to your FastAPI backend. • FastAPI queries the database, gets back rows. • Pydantic serializes those rows into a JSON response. • JSON travels across the network. • JavaScript parses the JSON back into objects. • Those objects get stored in local state (or a cache layer like React Query). • React re-renders the component with the actual data. • Your table component maps over the array and produces DOM nodes. Ten steps. Ten places something can go wrong, add latency, or accumulate cost. And almost nobody questions this pipeline because it’s what everyone does — it’s the water the industry swims in. I want to make the case that steps 3 through 8 are, in a large category of B2B applications, entirely optional. Not “could be optimized” optional. Don’t need to exist optional. The Serialization Tax Nobody Talks About The issue isn’t that JSON is slow. JSON parsing is fast. The issue is that the entire JSON round-trip represents a category error in your architecture: you are converting structured data into a string, sending that string across a wire, and then converting it back into structured data — solely so a JavaScript runtime can then convert that back into HTML. Your database already has the data in structured form. Your server already has it in memory after the query. The only reason it becomes JSON in the first place is to satisfy a client that speaks JSON, and the only reason the client speaks JSON is because it’s a JavaScript runtime that needs to own the rendering step. Remove the JavaScript runtime from the rendering step, and the entire translation layer disappears. What FastAPI + HTMX Actually Looks Like Here’s the same data table, built with FastAPI, Jinja2, and HTMX, with Logical Brutalism handling the token layer: # FastAPI route — returns HTML fragment, not JSON @router.get("/orders", response_class=HTMLResponse) async def get_orders( request: Request, status: str = "pending", db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db) ): orders = await order_service.fetch_by_status(db, status) return templates.TemplateResponse("partials/orders_table.html", { "request": request, "orders": orders }) {% for order in orders %} {{ order.id }} {{ order.customer }} {{ order.status }} {{ order.total | currency }} {% endfor %} User changes the filter. HTMX fires a GET request. FastAPI queries the database, Jinja2 renders the rows directly from the ORM objects, and the server sends back a raw HTML fragment — roughly 2–4KB of actual markup. HTMX swaps it into the DOM. Done. No JSON. No parsing. No state management. No re-render cycle. No component hydration. The data went from your database to the user’s screen in two hops instead of ten. The React Version, For Comparison # FastAPI route — returns JSON @router.get("/api/orders") async def get_orders( status: str = "pending", db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db) ): orders = await order_service.fetch_by_status(db, status) return [OrderSchema.from_orm(order) for order in orders]// React component const OrdersTable = () => { const [status, setStatus] = useState("pending"); const { data, isLoading } = useQuery({ queryKey: ["orders", status], queryFn: () => fetch(`/api/orders?status=${status}`) .then(res => res.json()) }); if (isLoading) return ; return ( {data?.map(order => ( ))}
{order.id} {order.customer} {order.status} {formatCurrency(order.total)}
); }; This is clean code. It’s well-structured, readable, the kind of thing that passes code review without a second look. The problem isn’t the code — the problem is everything the code implies: a Pydantic schema to define the JSON shape, a React Query setup to manage caching and loading states, a TypeScript type to match the schema on the client, a skeleton component for the loading state, and a JavaScript bundle large enough to run all of it in the browser. None of those things solve a user’s problem. They solve the problem that arises from choosing a client-side rendering architecture — which is itself the choice you’re now paying to support. This Isn’t About HTMX Being “Simple” The criticism I hear most often about this approach is that it’s fine for simple CRUD apps but breaks down at scale, for complex interactivity, for “real” applications. I want to push back on that framing, because it mislocates where the complexity lives. The FastAPI + HTMX stack isn’t simple because it does less. It’s simpler because it doesn’t introduce accidental complexity — complexity that exists to manage other complexity you introduced earlier. A JSON API exists to serve a JavaScript client. A JavaScript client exists because you chose client-side rendering. Client-side rendering requires state management. State management requires cache invalidation logic. Cache invalidation logic is famously the second-hardest problem in computer science. Remove the first decision, and you remove the cascade it generates. HTMX handles modals, live search, partial page updates, optimistic UI, infinite scroll, and WebSocket-driven updates. It does all of this with hx- attributes on your HTML and a server that knows how to return fragments — no client-side routing, no hydration, no bundle splitting strategy required. Where the Trade-off Actually Lives I won’t pretend this architecture is a universal drop-in replacement for React. It isn’t, and I don’t want to oversell it. If your application has complex client-side state that genuinely doesn’t map to server requests — a rich text editor, a drag-and-drop canvas, a real-time collaborative document — then a JavaScript runtime owning that interaction is the right call. Some things live in the client because they belong in the client. But the vast majority of B2B dashboards I’ve seen don’t have that problem. They have tables, filters, forms, charts, and status indicators — all of which are fundamentally data-in-to-HTML-out pipelines where the server is the authoritative source of truth. Routing that through a JavaScript state machine in the browser doesn’t add capability to those interactions. It adds infrastructure to support the capability you already had when the server had the data in the first place. The question worth asking before you scaffold your next React component isn’t “how do I build this?” It’s “does this interaction actually require a JavaScript runtime to render the result?” Most of the time, the honest answer is no. What does not resolve, does not exist.
